revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Ishikawa no Iratsume]][[ja:石川内命婦]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|female|qid=Q6581072|1=Female (gender)}}|occupation={{ill|poet|qid=Q49757|1=Poet}}|spouse={{ill|Yasumaro Otomo|qid=Q11432337|1=Otomo no Otomaro}}|children={{ill|Inakimi Otomo|qid=Q11432352|1=Ōtomo no Inamaro}}; {{ill|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|qid=Q839976|1=Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume}}}}
{{Unreferenced|date=May 18, 2015 (Monday) 14:57 (UTC)}} '''Ishikawa Uchimyōfu''' (石川 内命婦, Ishikawa no Uchimyōfu, [[Dates of birth and death unknown]]) was a woman of the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}. She was the wife of {{ill|Ōtomo no Anamaro|ja|大伴安麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Anamaro|1=Otomo no Otomaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11432337}} and mother to {{ill|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|en|Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|qid=Q839976|lt=Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume|1=Ōtomo no Sakanoue no Iratsume}} and {{ill|Ōtomo no Inukimi|ja|大伴稲公|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Inukimi|1=Ōtomo no Inamaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11432352}}. She is also referred to as '''Ishikawa Myōfu''' (石川 命婦). Additionally, the poet '''Ishikawa no Iratsume''' (石川 郎女), who appears in the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|qid=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}}'', is sometimes identified as the same person, although there is no definitive consensus. She is also known as '''Ishikawa no Iratsume''' (石川 女郎) or '''Ōnako''' (大名児).
This article combines these accounts.
== Overview == Ishikawa Uchimyōfu's life, beyond her marriage to Ōtomo no Anamaro and her role as mother to Inukimi and Sakanoue no Iratsume, is not well-documented. She had a sister, {{ill|Azumi no Sotomyōfu|ja|安曇外命婦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azumi no Sotomyōfu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who was the mother of {{ill|Abe no Mushimaro|ja|阿倍虫麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abe no Mushimaro|1=Mushimaro Abe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q24874160}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|author=Tomiyoshi Kawakami|date=March 1975|url=https://otsuma.repo.nii.ac.jp/records/1692|title=The Legend of Ishikawa no Iratsume: Poems of Clan Women and Myōfu|journal=Otsuma Kokubun|issue=6|pages=104–pp6|publisher=Otsuma Women's University Literature Society|CRID=1520290885109758464}}</ref>
In {{ill|714|en|714|qid=Q23525|lt=714|1=714}} (Wadō 7), after the death of Anamaro, she likely assumed the role of ''Ōtoji'' (大刀自), the matriarch managing the Ōtomo family. A note accompanying a poem by Sakanoue no Iratsume in ''Man'yōshū'' (Volume 3-461) in {{ill|735|en|735|qid=Q23576|lt=735|1=735}} (Tenpyō 7) states, "The matriarch Ishikawa Myōfu visited the Arima Hot Springs for treatment." Additionally, a poem responding to an imperial decree from {{ill|Empress Genshō|en|Empress Genshō|qid=Q235343|lt=Empress Genshō|1=Empress Genshō}} in {{ill|733|en|733|qid=Q23570|lt=733|1=733}} (Tenpyō 5) survives (''Man'yōshū'', Volume 20-4439). If this is indeed the same person, she lived at least until that time.
On the other hand, Ishikawa no Iratsume is associated with romantic poems involving multiple men, such as {{ill|Prince Ōtsu|en|Prince Ōtsu|qid=Q1284081|lt=Prince Ōtsu|1=Prince Ōtsu}} and {{ill|Prince Kusakabe|en|Prince Kusakabe|qid=Q1046438|lt=Prince Kusakabe|1=Prince Kusakabe}}. Some older interpretations, such as that of {{ill|Tachibana no Moribe|ja|橘守部|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachibana no Moribe|1=Tachibana Moribe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q6536628}}, viewed her as a "wandering woman" (遊行女婦也, ''yūgyō nyofu'') based on ''Man'yōshū Hitsuji no Tsumade''. Modern scholarship, however, regards her as a woman from the powerful {{ill|Ishikawa clan|en|Ishikawa clan|qid=Q615039|lt=Ishikawa clan|1=Ishikawa clan}}, a branch of the {{ill|Soga clan|en|Soga clan|qid=Q909854|lt=Soga clan|1=Soga clan}} based in Ishikawa, Takichi District, Yamato Province. Her connection to Ōtsu and Kusakabe is attributed to the Ishikawa clan's maternal ties to them (their mothers were daughters of {{ill|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|en|Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro|qid=Q3488899|lt=Soga no Kurayamada Ishikawa Maro|1=Soga no Kurayamada no Ishikawa no Maro}} and Princess Tochi, a daughter of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|qid=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji|1=Emperor Tenji}}).
According to ''Man'yōshū'', Iratsume was initially a maid of honor to Prince Ōtsu (referred to as ''mikatachi'' in the preface to Poem 129, Volume 2). She was involved in a love triangle with Kusakabe and Ōtsu. The preface to Poem 108 states that she was secretly courted by Ōtsu, which implies an illicit affair while serving Kusakabe.
Her poetic exchanges with {{ill|Kume no Zenji|ja|久米禅師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kume no Zenji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} are set in Emperor Tenji's era, predating her interactions with Ōtsu and Kusakabe. Given the significant age differences between Iratsume and these figures, there is debate over whether all references pertain to the same person. Moreover, she exchanged ''sōmonka'' (相聞歌, love poems) with {{ill|Ōtomo no Tadami|ja|大伴田主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Tadami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Ōtomo no Sukunamaro|ja|大伴宿奈麻呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtomo no Sukunamaro|1=Ōtomo no Sukinamaro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10933541}}, further complicating interpretations of her identity.
These complexities have led scholars to view "Ishikawa no Iratsume" as a generic title meaning "daughter of the Ishikawa clan," referring to multiple individuals (e.g., {{ill|Mizue Aso|ja|阿蘇瑞枝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizue Aso|1=Mizue Aso|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q27892406}}). Additionally, the playful nature of the poems, such as the prose commentary attached to Poem 126, suggests a less serious intent.
== Man'yōshū Poems Related to Ishikawa no Iratsume and Uchimyōfu == The following five poems involve her interactions with Kume no Zenji:
* Kume no Zenji's courtship poems to Ishikawa no Iratsume: ''' As the Shinanome bow is strung with cords, will the noble say no? (Zenji) [Volume 2-95] ''' Without stringing the Shinanome bow, you claim to know its workings. (Iratsume) [Volume 2-96] ''' Though I bend the catalpa bow as I wish, the heart behind it I cannot discern. (Iratsume) [Volume 2-97] ''' The skilled archer, stringing the bow, knows the heart of his craft. (Zenji) [Volume 2-98] ''' Like the lid of an Eastern box, my heart rides upon your thoughts. (Zenji) [Volume 2-99]
These four poems involve Ōtsu, Iratsume, and Kusakabe:
* A poem by Prince Ōtsu to Ishikawa no Iratsume: ''' Standing in the mountain mist for you, I am drenched. [Volume 2-107] * Ishikawa no Iratsume's reply: ''' To stand in the mountain mist for me—I would have met you. [Volume 2-108] * On Ōtsu's secret liaison with Iratsume, exposed by the divination of Tsunori: ''' To know our secret liaison, the diviner betrayed me. [Volume 2-109] * A poem by Kusakabe to Iratsume: ''' I shall never forget you, as surely as I bundle the grass of distant fields. [Volume 2-pp0]
In her later years, known as Ishikawa Uchimyōfu, she composed the following poem under imperial command to pray for {{ill|Princess Minushi|en|Princess Minushi|qid=Q7244760|lt=Mizushiro no Ōji|1=Princess Minushi}}, who had fallen ill:
* In winter, as snow fell heavily upon the land, she composed: ''' The snow reaches the earth like pine branches—will you see it from your seclusion? [Volume 20-4439]
== References == {{Reflist}}
